logger.logger_name: Adirondack Timber & Chipping, LLC – Attn: Michael J. Carver

forest_management__operations.forest_management_objective:
The primary objective of the proposed forest management activities on the Whitaker parcel at 145 Evergreen Point Road in Hague, NY is to conduct an uneven-aged selective timber harvest that improves long‑term forest health, enhances wildlife habitat, and reduces fuel loading and wildfire risk, while generating a modest and sustainable income stream for the landowner.

This 46‑acre property has not been commercially harvested in more than 35 years. The overstory is currently dominated by mature red maple, sugar maple, and red oak with scattered white pine and hemlock. There is a noticeable component of declining, over‑mature hardwoods, areas of ice and wind damage from storms in 2019 and 2020, and several pockets of beech with significant beech bark disease. The stand is showing signs of stagnation in certain portions, with limited light reaching the forest floor and a sparse regeneration layer of desirable species.

Specific management objectives are:
1. Forest health and resiliency: Remove diseased, poorly formed, and storm‑damaged trees (particularly diseased beech and declining red maple) to reduce competition and allow vigorous crop trees—primarily sugar maple, red oak, and quality white pine—to respond with increased crown development and diameter growth. The goal is to leave a well‑spaced, structurally diverse stand better able to withstand future wind, ice, pest, and disease events.

2. Wildlife habitat improvement: Retain and, where appropriate, create cavity trees, snags, and coarse woody debris to support songbirds, small mammals, and amphibians. A portion of the low‑value pulpwood and tops will be left in the woods in strategic locations to increase downed woody material. Small canopy gaps (0.1–0.25 acres) will be created in a few areas to encourage regeneration of soft mast species and to provide early successional habitat for species such as ruffed grouse and woodcock.

3. Water quality and soil conservation: Design all skid trails, landings, and any limited new truck access using contour‑following alignments, adequate drainage, and protective buffers along all intermittent and perennial drainages. The property slopes generally west toward Lake George, and a key objective is to ensure that no sediment or logging debris reaches the lake or any tributary swales. The harvest is being planned with the explicit intent of meeting or exceeding the Lake George Park Commission’s silviculture exemption standards and New York State forestry best management practices.

4. Aesthetics and recreational use: The landowner and his family use the woods for hiking, cross‑country skiing, and occasional deer hunting. The harvest will maintain a natural, uneven‑aged appearance, avoiding large clearcuts. Residual trees of good form and crown condition will be favored along existing informal footpaths, and visual buffers will be maintained within approximately 150 feet of the existing seasonal camp and along the most frequently used trail. Slash will be lopped low in these high‑use areas to reduce visual impact and improve walkability.

5. Sustainable timber production: Remove approximately 35–45 MBF of sawtimber (mixed hardwoods and some pine) and 60–80 cords of pulp/firewood on a one‑time entry, with the expectation that a follow‑up thinning or improvement cut could be considered in 15–20 years. Trees will be marked by a NYS‑certified forester (Laura M. Kensington) to ensure that residual basal area remains within recommended guidelines for uneven‑aged northern hardwood stands.

In summary, the objective is not a short‑term liquidation harvest but a carefully planned improvement cut that balances ecological, recreational, and economic values while rigorously protecting Lake George’s water quality.
forest_management__operations.best_management_practices_to_be_utilized:
The following best management practices (BMPs) will be implemented on site to protect water quality, with particular attention to maintaining the exceptional clarity and quality of Lake George and its tributaries. Many of these are drawn directly from the NYS Forestry BMP Field Guide and tailored to the specific topography and soils on this parcel.

1. Riparian buffers and filter strips:
- A minimum 100‑foot no‑equipment buffer will be maintained from the ordinary high‑water mark of Lake George. Within this buffer, only limited single‑tree selection (hazard or safety concerns) will be allowed, and all equipment will remain outside the buffer.
- Along the two intermittent drainages that run toward the lake (identified and flagged by the forester during layout), a 50‑foot equipment‑free buffer will be maintained on each side where topography allows. Slash will not be placed in these drainages, and no skid trails will cross them except at pre‑designated, stabilized locations.

2. Skid trail layout and design:
- Primary skid trails will be flagged in advance by the forester to follow the contour as much as possible, avoiding fall‑line trails that could concentrate runoff.
- Grades on main skid trails will be kept under 15% wherever feasible, and short steeper pitches will include additional water diversion structures (water bars, turnouts).
- Existing old woods roads will be used where practical to minimize new disturbance, with improvements such as grading, out‑sloping, and seeding after use.

3. Erosion and sediment control measures:
- Water bars and broad‑based dips will be installed on all skid trails and any temporary truck roads at spacing consistent with slope (e.g., approximately every 50 feet on steeper sections, 75–100 feet on gentler slopes), following NYS BMP guidelines.
- Disturbed soil on landings and heavily used trail segments will be stabilized at the end of operations using a seed mix suitable for the Adirondack region (e.g., conservation mix with ryegrass and clover) and clean straw mulch. The landowner has agreed to check seeded areas periodically during the first growing season and to reseed if needed.
- Where skid trails approach drainages or low areas, out‑sloping and turn‑outs will be used to disperse water into vegetated areas before it can reach any channel.

4. Stream and drainage crossings:
- No direct crossings of Lake George are proposed.
- One intermittent drainage may need to be crossed by a skid trail. If this crossing is used, a temporary portable bridge (such as timber mats or a skidder bridge) will be installed to keep equipment out of the channel and off the banks. Approaches will be armored with slash or small stone if necessary to reduce rutting.
- The temporary crossing will be removed immediately upon completion of skidding in that portion of the sale, and disturbed soil at the approaches will be stabilized with seed and mulch.

5. Equipment operation and fuel handling:
- All equipment (skidder, loader, and trucks) will be well maintained and checked daily for fluid leaks before entering the site. Any leaking equipment will be repaired offsite before being returned to the job.
- Fueling and equipment maintenance will occur only at the designated landing area, which is located more than 300 feet from Lake George and outside of any drainage swale.
- Spill kits (absorbent pads, booms, and disposal bags) will be maintained on each piece of equipment and at the landing. The crew has been instructed in spill response procedures, and any spill greater than minor drips will be reported and cleaned up immediately.

6. Timing of operations and wet‑weather restrictions:
- The intent is to operate during relatively dry or frozen ground conditions. Skidding will be suspended if rutting exceeds approximately 6 inches in depth over extended distances or if there is visible muddy runoff leaving the trail system.
- No skidding will be allowed directly after heavy rainfall events until soils have had time to drain and firm up. The forester and logger will communicate at least weekly regarding site conditions and will adjust work scheduling accordingly.

7. Landing design and log deck management:
- The primary log landing will be located on a gently sloping, previously disturbed area near Evergreen Point Road, well away from any drainage to Lake George.
- The landing will be graded to shed water into stable, vegetated areas rather than toward any ditch or watercourse.
- Bark, slash, and other debris will be periodically pushed and spread in a thin layer within the landing perimeter to help reduce bare soil exposure. At the conclusion of operations, compacted soils will be lightly scarified, seeded, and mulched.

8. Slash management and visual buffers near the lake:
- Within approximately 200 feet of the lakeshore, slash will be lopped and scattered to a height of 2 feet or less to reduce visual impact and to avoid creating barriers to foot travel.
- No tops or logging debris will be allowed to enter Lake George or any drainage channel. Any accidental deposition will be removed immediately.

9. Protection of existing roads and ditches:
- Evergreen Point Road is a private, gravel camp road with shallow ditches. Truck traffic will be limited to periods when the road is firm and not saturated.
- Any tracking of mud onto the road will be periodically scraped and returned to the landing area. Roadside ditches will be inspected after major haul periods, and any disturbed areas will be stabilized with seed and mulch as necessary.

10. Monitoring and post‑harvest review:
- The forester (Laura M. Kensington) will periodically visit the site during active operations to confirm that BMPs are being followed and to make adjustments as needed based on actual field conditions.
- After completion of harvesting and close‑out work (water bars, seeding, etc.), the landowner and forester will walk the skid trail network and landing to verify that there is no evidence of erosion or sediment delivery toward Lake George. Any problem areas discovered will be addressed promptly with additional stabilization measures.

Collectively, these BMPs are intended to ensure that soil disturbance is minimized, runoff is controlled and dispersed through vegetated areas, and Lake George and its tributaries remain fully protected during and after the logging operation.
forest_management__operations.schedule_for_road_building_and_harvestin:
The proposed schedule has been designed to take advantage of favorable ground conditions (either frozen or relatively dry) and to avoid peak runoff periods as much as reasonably possible. The timing below is approximate and may shift slightly based on weather, contractor availability, and ground conditions, but the general sequence and seasonal windows will be followed.

1. Pre‑harvest planning and layout (2025‑05‑15 to 2025‑06‑30):
- By approximately 2025‑05‑15, the landowner and forester will have finalized the timber sale contract with Adirondack Timber & Chipping, LLC.
- Between 2025‑05‑20 and 2025‑06‑30, the forester will complete detailed stand inventory, mark trees to be harvested with paint, and flag the main skid trails, landing location, and any potential stream/drainage crossing. Riparian buffers and no‑cut areas will also be flagged at this time.
- During this period, there will be minimal ground disturbance, limited mainly to foot travel by the forester and landowner.

2. Road and landing preparation (2025‑07‑10 to 2025‑07‑20):
- In mid‑July 2025, assuming soils are reasonably dry, the logger will mobilize equipment to the site over a 2–3 day period.
- The existing woods access from Evergreen Point Road will be lightly improved: brushing back encroaching vegetation, minor grading, and placement of a small amount of crushed stone if needed near the entrance to prevent rutting.
- The primary log landing (approximately 0.25–0.33 acres) will be brushed, leveled as needed, and shaped to drain away from any ditches or drainages. No significant cut‑and‑fill work is anticipated.

3. Skid trail establishment (2025‑07‑20 to 2025‑08‑05):
- Over roughly two weeks, the main skid trails will be opened using the skidder, following the pre‑flagged routes. Care will be taken to avoid unnecessary trail proliferation.
- As trails are opened, the operator will install basic drainage features (out‑sloping, initial water bars on steeper segments) so that the trail system is functional and stable from the outset.

4. Primary harvesting period (2025‑08‑05 to 2025‑10‑15):
- The bulk of felling, skidding, and decking of logs is expected to occur between early August and mid‑October 2025.
- Work will typically proceed 4–5 days per week when weather and ground conditions are suitable. Operations will be reduced or temporarily halted during prolonged wet periods or immediately after heavy rain events to avoid rutting and erosion.
- The forester will visit the site approximately every 2–3 weeks during this period to review progress, confirm adherence to BMPs, and adjust marking or trail routing if unexpected conditions arise (e.g., soft spots, seeps).

5. Final harvesting and cleanup (2025‑10‑15 to 2025‑11‑15):
- By mid‑October, the majority of marked timber should be removed. The remaining time will be used to finish any small pockets not yet harvested, to pull temporary stream/drainage crossings, and to close out skid trails and the landing.
- Water bars and broad‑based dips will be installed or reinforced on all skid trails. Disturbed soils on steep sections, at the landing, and at any former crossings will be seeded with a conservation mix and mulched with clean straw.
- All merchantable wood will be trucked offsite, and unneeded slash or debris at the landing will be spread and incorporated lightly into the soil surface to aid in stabilization.

6. Contingency for winter operations (if needed; 2026‑01‑05 to 2026‑03‑01):
- If weather or market conditions delay a portion of the work, the remaining harvesting may be completed during the winter of 2026 under frozen ground conditions. In that case, operations would resume around 2026‑01‑05, focusing on areas that were not accessible under soft summer/fall conditions.
- Winter skidding would further reduce soil disturbance in any wetter sections of the property. BMPs for snow and ice conditions (e.g., maintaining water control structures as snow melts) would be followed.

7. Post‑harvest monitoring (through 2026‑10‑31):
- During the first growing season following completion of the harvest (spring through fall 2026), the landowner and forester will walk the skid trail network and landing at least twice (once in late spring/early summer and once in early fall) to verify that seeded areas have established and that no rills, gullies, or sediment movement toward Lake George is occurring.
- If any problem areas are identified (e.g., failed seedings, developing erosion features), additional seeding, mulching, or water control structures will be installed as needed.

Overall, active road building and harvesting operations are expected to be concentrated between mid‑July and mid‑November 2025, with potential follow‑up or contingency work in winter 2026 if conditions require it. The schedule has been intentionally structured to allow flexibility to pause operations during unfavorable weather while still completing the work within a reasonable timeframe and maintaining strong protections for soil and water resources.
